## Must-do activities in Hong Kong for a first visit

For your first Hong Kong experience, start by exploring the bustling Central district. Stroll down Hollywood Road, known for its art galleries and antique shops, before hopping on the iconic double-decker tram to reach Victoria Peak. At the summit, enjoy a breathtaking panoramic view of the city and harbor. In the evening, don’t miss the “Symphony of Lights” sound and light show that illuminates the skyscrapers of Hong Kong Island and Kowloon. Food enthusiasts should definitely check out the street markets and traditional dim sum restaurants to sample the local flavors. [...] For your first trip to Hong Kong, explore beyond the iconic sites and discover its hidden treasures. Start with the Mong Kok Flower Market, a colorful peaceful haven away from the urban hustle. Wander through the fragrant stalls and admire rare orchids, starting at $5 per bouquet. Continue to the Tin Hau Temple in Yau Ma Tei, an architectural gem dedicated to the goddess of the sea. Its serene atmosphere and detailed murals offer a fascinating glimpse into local culture. For a unique culinary experience, try the dai pai dong Sing Heung Yuen in the Central district, famous for its tomato noodles at just $4 per bowl. [...] For a memorable culinary experience, visit a dai pai dong, these typical street food stalls. Try the delicious roast pork and wonton noodles for about $9. Explore the Sham Shui Po district, less touristy but full of gastronomic treasures and discount electronics stores. For a panoramic view at a lower cost, take the Star Ferry ($0.60) between Kowloon and Hong Kong Island at sunset. While you’re there, stroll along the Avenue of Stars and enjoy the “Symphony of Lights” sound and light show at 8pm.

## First Visit to Hong Kong: Everything You Need to Know

For a first visit to Hong Kong, thorough preparation is essential to make the most of this fascinating destination. Start by familiarizing yourself with iconic neighborhoods like Central, Tsim Sha Tsui, and Mong Kok, each offering a unique experience. Consider getting an Octopus Card upon arrival, which is indispensable for public transportation and many purchases. Hong Kong’s subtropical climate can be unpredictable, so pack clothing suitable for various conditions. Don’t forget to check if a visa is required for your stay, although many countries enjoy visa exemptions for short visits.

Hong Kong’s cuisine is a must-try during your trip. Dare to sample local specialties at dai pai dong, these typical street food stalls, or in Michelin-starred restaurants for a high-end culinary experience. To fully enjoy the spectacular views, take the funicular to the Peak or cross the bay on the Star Ferry. Shopping enthusiasts will find their happy place in the bustling markets of Temple Street or Ladies’ Market. To escape the urban excitement, explore hiking trails in the New Territories or secluded beaches on the nearby islands.

To make the most of your stay, schedule your visits according to opening hours and crowd levels. Hong Kong Park and the Chi Lin Monastery offer peaceful retreats in the heart of the city. For a cultural immersion, attend a Cantonese opera performance or visit the Hong Kong Museum of History. Since public transportation is efficient, avoid renting a car. Finally, stay alert to tourist scams, especially in crowded areas. By following these tips, your first trip to Hong Kong will be an unforgettable experience, blending tradition and modernity in a setting that’s unique in the world.

## First time itinerary in Hong Kong: optimal day-by-day plan

For a first trip to Hong Kong, a 5-day itinerary allows you to discover the essentials of this fascinating metropolis. Start by exploring the bustling Central district, with its iconic skyscrapers and unique blend of modernity and tradition. Then head to the top of Victoria Peak for a breathtaking panoramic view of the city and harbor. In the evening, don’t miss the “Symphony of Lights” sound and light show illuminating the buildings. On the second day, immerse yourself in the authentic atmosphere of the bird market and flower market in Mong Kok, before strolling along the Avenue of Stars and admiring the sunset over the bay.

On the third day, take the ferry to discover Lantau Island and its impressive Tian Tan Buddha. While there, visit the picturesque stilt houses in the fishing village of Tai O. Back on the main island, treat yourself to a relaxing evening at the trendy bars in the SoHo district. On the fourth day, immerse yourself in local history at the Hong Kong Museum of History, then explore the vibrant Wong Tai Sin Temple. Finish with a stroll through Kowloon Park to watch locals practicing tai chi. For your final day, treat yourself to a getaway on Cheung Chau Island, known for its beaches and fresh seafood.

This balanced itinerary will allow you to experience the many facets of Hong Kong, from its must-see attractions to its hidden treasures. Feel free to adjust the order of visits depending on the weather and your preferences. To optimize your travel around the city, get a rechargeable Octopus card, which can be used on public transportation and at many businesses. Also consider booking popular attractions like the Ngong Ping 360 cable car in advance to avoid long lines. Finally, for an authentic culinary experience, don’t be afraid to try local restaurants and street markets, while being sure to follow basic hygiene practices. With these insider tips, your first trip to Hong Kong will be unforgettable.

## Must-do activities in Hong Kong for a first visit

For your first Hong Kong experience, start by exploring the bustling Central district. Stroll down Hollywood Road, known for its art galleries and antique shops, before hopping on the iconic double-decker tram to reach Victoria Peak. At the summit, enjoy a breathtaking panoramic view of the city and harbor. In the evening, don’t miss the “Symphony of Lights” sound and light show that illuminates the skyscrapers of Hong Kong Island and Kowloon. Food enthusiasts should definitely check out the street markets and traditional dim sum restaurants to sample the local flavors.

Continue your adventure by heading to Lantau Island, accessible by ferry or subway. Visit the Tian Tan Big Buddha and Po Lin Monastery, iconic sites that offer a striking contrast to the urban buzz. For a more authentic experience, make a stop at the fishing village of Tai O, known for its stilt houses and traditional way of life. Back on the main island, treat yourself to a relaxing break in the Nan Lian Gardens, a peaceful haven in the heart of the city, before immersing yourself in the nightlife of the Lan Kwai Fong district, famous for its trendy bars and restaurants.

Complete your stay with a sampan boat tour in Aberdeen Harbor, a remnant of Hong Kong’s maritime past. Then, head to the Yuen Po Street Bird Garden and the Prince Edward Flower Market to discover unique aspects of local culture. Shopping enthusiasts won’t want to miss the night markets at Temple Street and Ladies’ Market in Mong Kok, where you can find everything at attractive prices. Finally, for a one-of-a-kind experience, take the Star Ferry at sunset to cross Victoria Harbor and admire Hong Kong’s iconic skyline as it gradually lights up, offering an unforgettable spectacle that perfectly concludes your first journey to this fascinating metropolis.

## Best neighborhoods in Hong Kong for your first visit

For your first visit to Hong Kong, focus on the iconic neighborhoods that offer a complete overview of this fascinating city. Central, the financial heart, is a must-see with its impressive skyscrapers and blend of modernity and tradition. Explore the lively streets of SoHo, taste local cuisine in the night markets, and admire the panoramic view from the Peak. Don’t miss Tsim Sha Tsui, on the Kowloon peninsula, for its picturesque waterfront and Avenue of Stars. This district also offers a breathtaking view of Hong Kong Island’s skyline, particularly spectacular during the Symphony of Lights sound and light show.

Mong Kok, in northern Kowloon, will immerse you in the excitement of local life. Its colorful markets, like the flower market and bird market, offer unique experiences. For a striking contrast, visit the trendy Sheung Wan district, where design boutiques sit alongside traditional temples. Art lovers will appreciate the West Kowloon district, with its numerous galleries and the new M+ museum. For a more peaceful getaway, Lantau Island offers quiet beaches and the famous Tian Tan Buddha, accessible by a scenic cable car.

To make the most of your first visit, plan to spend at least 4-5 days exploring these essential neighborhoods. Use the efficient MTR subway system to easily move between areas. Don’t be afraid to get lost in the side streets where you’ll discover hidden treasures. Sample local cuisine at dai pai dong, these authentic street food stalls. For a unique experience, take the Star Ferry at sunset between Tsim Sha Tsui and Central. Finally, be sure to soak in the atmosphere of urban parks like Hong Kong Park or Kowloon Park, peaceful havens in the heart of this dynamic metropolis.

## Where to stay during your first time in Hong Kong?

For your first trip to Hong Kong, choosing the right accommodation is crucial. The neighborhoods of Tsim Sha Tsui and Central are ideal for first-time visitors, offering easy access to major attractions and public transportation. Tsim Sha Tsui, located on the Kowloon Peninsula, features breathtaking views of Victoria Harbor and a vibrant atmosphere, while Central, on Hong Kong Island, serves as the financial and cultural heart of the city. Hotels in these areas vary in price and style, ranging from luxury establishments to more affordable options, making it possible to find accommodations suited to any budget.

For an authentic experience, consider staying in the Mong Kok neighborhood. This bustling area offers total immersion in local culture, with its street markets, shops, and renowned street food. If you prefer a quieter environment, Lantau Island is an excellent option, particularly around Mui Wo or Tai O. These areas offer a more relaxed atmosphere and are ideal for exploring the surrounding nature. Don’t forget to check online reviews and book in advance, especially if you’re traveling during peak seasons.

Whatever you decide, prioritize accommodations near an MTR (subway) station to make getting around easier. Youth hostels and short-term rental apartments are interesting alternatives to traditional hotels, often offering better value for money and a more immersive experience. To maximize your stay, choose lodging that matches your interests: close to historical sites if you’re passionate about history, in a trendy neighborhood if you enjoy nightlife, or near hiking trails if you appreciate nature. With thoughtful planning, your first stay in Hong Kong will be unforgettable.

## Transport in Hong Kong: getting around easily upon arrival

In Hong Kong, newcomers quickly discover an efficient and modern transportation network. The Octopus Card, a rechargeable card, is the key to moving around the city with ease. Available at the airport, it allows you to use the subway (MTR), buses, trams, and even ferries. For first-time visitors, it’s recommended to purchase one upon arrival to fully enjoy the diversity of transportation options. The Airport Express, a fast train connecting the airport to downtown in just 24 minutes, offers a first glimpse into the efficiency of Hong Kong’s transportation system.

The MTR, the backbone of the network, serves most neighborhoods with punctuality and high frequency. Stations are clearly marked in English and Chinese, making it easy for travelers to find their way around. For an authentic experience, the iconic double-decker trams, called “Ding Ding” by locals, offer a unique perspective of the city at a modest price. Double-decker buses, meanwhile, are ideal for exploring areas less accessible by subway, such as Lantau Island or the New Territories.

To optimize your travel, the MTR mobile app provides real-time routes and connection information. Taxis, easily identifiable by their colors (red for Hong Kong Island and Kowloon, green for the New Territories), are a practical option for short or late-night trips. An insider tip: avoid rush hours (8-9:30 AM and 5:30-7 PM) for a more comfortable journey. Finally, don’t hesitate to use the Mid-Levels escalators, the world’s longest outdoor covered escalator system, for a unique and panoramic transportation experience in the heart of Central.

## Our budget tips for a first visit to Hong Kong: actual costs and money-saving tips

For your first trip to Hong Kong, it’s essential to carefully plan your budget. Costs can quickly add up in this dynamic metropolis, but with a few tips, it’s possible to make substantial savings. Accommodation typically represents the largest expense, with average prices ranging from $55 to $165 per night depending on the level of comfort you’re seeking. To reduce this cost, opt for less central neighborhoods like Kowloon or New Territories, where rates are more affordable. The efficient and inexpensive public transportation system will allow you to easily get around the entire city for about $5 to $11 per day.

Food in Hong Kong offers excellent value for money, especially if you focus on street food and local restaurants. Budget around $16 to $27 per day for your meals. Tourist attractions can add up, but many iconic sites like Victoria Peak or Wong Tai Sin Temple are free or inexpensive. For paid attractions, consider purchasing a tourist pass that offers significant discounts. Avoid peak seasons like Chinese New Year to benefit from better rates on accommodations and flights. Plan for a total budget of about $85 to $130 per day for a comfortable stay without missing out on experiences.

To maximize your savings, explore night markets like Temple Street for affordable souvenirs. Take advantage of happy hours at bars to enjoy cocktails at lower prices. Use a credit card with no foreign transaction fees to avoid bank charges. Don’t hesitate to haggle in small shops, as it’s a common practice in Hong Kong. Finally, download local apps like OpenRice to find the best budget-friendly dining spots. With these insider tips, you can fully enjoy your first visit to Hong Kong while keeping your budget in check. This fascinating city offers a unique experience, blending tradition and modernity, that will leave a lasting impression in your travel memories.

## Gastronomy in Hong Kong: where to eat for your first time?

For your first trip to Hong Kong, the local cuisine is an absolute must-try. This cosmopolitan city is packed with diverse culinary options, from street food stalls to Michelin-starred restaurants. Start by exploring the night markets like Temple Street, where you can sample typical dishes such as beef noodles or fish balls starting at around $5. Don’t miss out on dim sum either, those small steamed bites served in bamboo baskets, a Cantonese specialty best enjoyed in traditional restaurants like Lin Heung Tea House.

For a more refined experience, head to the Central district, where many renowned establishments are located. The restaurant Lung King Heen, with its three Michelin stars, offers exceptional Cantonese cuisine in an elegant setting, with tasting menus starting at $165. If you prefer a more casual atmosphere, go for Little Bao, an original Asian burger concept starting at $16. Don’t forget to try the Hong Kong-style milk tea, an iconic local beverage that you can find in most cafes throughout the city.

To complete your culinary journey, head to Lantau Island where you can enjoy fresh seafood at the waterfront restaurants in Tai O fishing village. The prices there are more affordable, with generous meals costing around $30 per person. Finally, for a breathtaking view of the city while savoring modern fusion cuisine, Café Gray Deluxe on the 49th floor of The Upper House hotel offers an unforgettable experience, with main dishes priced around $60. Whatever your preference, Hong Kong will surely satisfy your taste buds during your first visit.

## Mistakes to absolutely avoid during your first visit to Hong Kong

For your first trip to Hong Kong, avoid common mistakes that could spoil your experience. Don’t underestimate the importance of comprehensive travel insurance that covers medical expenses and cancellations. With the high population density, prepare yourself for the urban bustle and fast-paced rhythm. Try to avoid traveling during rush hours if possible. Don’t neglect the language barrier either: although English is widespread, learning a few words in Cantonese will make your interactions easier. Finally, respect local customs, such as removing your shoes before entering someone’s home.

Hong Kong’s cuisine is renowned, but be cautious of unauthorized street vendors. Stick to recommended establishments or those displaying hygiene ratings. When shopping, watch out for counterfeit products in markets. For transportation, choose the Octopus Card, which is more convenient than individual tickets. Don’t rely solely on the subway: trams and ferries offer a unique city experience. Avoid scheduling too many activities per day, as Hong Kong is full of surprises that deserve your time. Remember to consider the weather, which is particularly humid and hot during summer.

When it comes to budget, Hong Kong can quickly become expensive. Plan for a comfortable margin and avoid tourist areas for your meals and shopping. For accommodations, book in advance as prices fluctuate rapidly. Be wary of taxi scams: insist on using the meter. During your visits, don’t limit yourself to Hong Kong Island. Kowloon and the New Territories offer a different perspective of the city. Finally, stay vigilant with your personal belongings in crowded places. By following these tips, you’ll maximize your chances of having an unforgettable first stay in this fascinating Asian metropolis.

## What Locals Actually Know About Hong Kong

For your first trip to Hong Kong, follow these insider tips to explore the city like a local. Start by getting an Octopus card, essential for public transportation and small purchases. Choose the double-decker trams, which are cheaper ($2.80) than the subway and offer an unbeatable view of the city. Discover the lively street markets like Temple Street, where you can taste authentic local dishes for less than $5.50. Don’t hesitate to venture into the hidden alleys of Sheung Wan to find unique shops and trendy cafes frequented by residents.

For a memorable culinary experience, visit a dai pai dong, these typical street food stalls. Try the delicious roast pork and wonton noodles for about $9. Explore the Sham Shui Po district, less touristy but full of gastronomic treasures and discount electronics stores. For a panoramic view at a lower cost, take the Star Ferry ($0.60) between Kowloon and Hong Kong Island at sunset. While you’re there, stroll along the Avenue of Stars and enjoy the “Symphony of Lights” sound and light show at 8pm.

Finally, escape the urban hustle by heading to Lantau Island. Take the Ngong Ping 360 cable car ($22 round-trip) to admire the Big Buddha and Po Lin Monastery. Continue your nature getaway by hiking the Dragon’s Back trail, offering spectacular views of the South China Sea. For a cultural immersion, attend a Cantonese opera performance at the Tea House Theatre in Xiqu Centre (starting at $16). These authentic experiences will allow you to discover Hong Kong from a unique perspective, far from the typical tourist paths.

## Beyond the classics: Hidden gems to discover in Hong Kong

For your first trip to Hong Kong, explore beyond the iconic sites and discover its hidden treasures. Start with the Mong Kok Flower Market, a colorful peaceful haven away from the urban hustle. Wander through the fragrant stalls and admire rare orchids, starting at $5 per bouquet. Continue to the Tin Hau Temple in Yau Ma Tei, an architectural gem dedicated to the goddess of the sea. Its serene atmosphere and detailed murals offer a fascinating glimpse into local culture. For a unique culinary experience, try the dai pai dong Sing Heung Yuen in the Central district, famous for its tomato noodles at just $4 per bowl.

Next, escape to Cheung Chau Island, accessible by ferry for $3 round-trip. This small car-free paradise is home to peaceful beaches and picturesque hiking trails. Don’t miss the Cheung Po Tsai Cave, the legendary hideout of an 18th-century pirate. For a breathtaking view of the city, take the funicular to the summit of High West Mountain, a less crowded alternative to the famous Victoria Peak. The ride costs $2 and rewards you with stunning panoramic views of the islands and skyscrapers. In the evening, explore the artistic neighborhood of Sham Shui Po, where local galleries and trendy cafes stand alongside traditional shops.

To wrap up your trip in style, treat yourself to a sunset cruise in Victoria Harbor. Local companies offer one-hour tours starting at $16 per person, often including a drink. It’s the perfect opportunity to admire the “Symphony of Lights” sound and light show from the water. Finally, don’t leave Hong Kong without trying egg waffles, a local specialty. The best place to enjoy them is Lee Keung Kee North Point Egg Waffles, an institution that offers them for just $2.25 each. These unique and affordable experiences will allow you to discover the authentic side of Hong Kong, off the beaten path.

## How to visit Hong Kong responsibly: A complete sustainable travel guide

To visit Hong Kong responsibly, start by prioritizing the city’s efficient public transportation. The MTR subway and iconic ferries offer an authentic experience while reducing your carbon footprint. Choose eco-friendly accommodations, such as hotels certified by the Hong Kong Green Hotel Award. These establishments implement sustainable practices, from waste management to energy conservation. For dining, select local restaurants that source seasonal products from regional farms. This way, you’ll support the local economy while discovering Hong Kong’s rich culinary heritage.

Explore the city on foot or by bike for a complete and eco-friendly immersion. The hiking trails of the New Territories offer spectacular views of the islands and the South China Sea. For shopping, favor traditional markets and local artisan shops, like those in the Sham Shui Po district. You’ll find unique and sustainable souvenirs, far from the international chain stores. Participate in responsible cultural activities, such as guided tours organized by local associations that donate their profits to community projects. These experiences will allow you to better understand Hong Kong culture while making a positive contribution to the local society.

Finally, respect the environment by following the conservation rules of natural parks and marine reserves. The Hong Kong Wetland Park and the Geopark offer unique opportunities to observe local wildlife and flora. Use a reusable water bottle and decline single-use plastics, which are ubiquitous in Hong Kong. For a positive long-term impact, consider participating in beach cleanups or tree planting activities organized by local NGOs. These concrete actions will allow you to leave a positive footprint on this fascinating metropolis. By adopting these responsible practices, you’ll help preserve Hong Kong’s natural and cultural treasures for future generations.

## The ultimate checklist for your first trip to Hong Kong

For your first visit to Hong Kong, a well-thought-out checklist is essential. Start by booking your flight and accommodation at least three months in advance to get the best rates. Plan for a budget of approximately $110 per day, excluding lodging. Make sure your passport is valid for at least six months beyond your return date and verify current entry requirements. Consider purchasing travel insurance that covers medical expenses and evacuation. Prepare a list of must-see sites like the Peak Tram, Avenue of Stars, and Wong Tai Sin Temple. Don’t forget to pack a universal power adapter and light, breathable clothing, as the climate is hot and humid for much of the year.

To get around efficiently, grab a rechargeable Octopus card as soon as you arrive at the airport. It allows you to use all public transportation and pay at many businesses. Download the MTR Mobile app to easily navigate the subway system. Be sure to pack comfortable walking shoes, as you’ll be exploring the city’s bustling streets extensively. Book popular attractions in advance, such as the Hong Kong Ferris Wheel or a Victoria Harbor cruise. For an authentic culinary experience, don’t hesitate to try the street markets and local restaurants. Remember to bring basic medications and a first aid kit, although pharmacies are plentiful throughout the city.

In terms of safety, Hong Kong is a secure destination, but stay alert in crowded areas. Keep digital and physical copies of important documents on your phone and at your hotel. For communication, English is widely spoken, though locals will appreciate if you learn a few Cantonese phrases. Don’t hesitate to ask your hotel staff for local recommendations. Budget time to explore less touristy neighborhoods like Sham Shui Po or Kowloon City. Finally, respect local customs, such as avoiding pointing at people or touching someone’s head. With this detailed checklist, you’re ready for an unforgettable first-time experience of Hong Kong, where tradition meets modernity in a unique urban setting.

Kong trip possible. So, let's go. [Music] Hi guys, my name is Angelica Wanders and this is Aileen 
Wanders and we create budget travel guides all around the world. On this channel, we share with 
you our easy to follow itineraries and also our cost and expenses for every trip. So, if you want 
to travel around the world in the easiest and most affordable way possible, then hit that like 
button and hit that subscribe button because we make videos just for you. Stick until the end of 
this video because we give you the ultimate 5-day budget itinerary and cost breakdown so you can get 
an idea of how much a Hong Kong trip could cost. And without further ado, let's explore Hong Kong. 
Let's start off with the basics. How do you score cheap flights to Hong Kong? Well, we usually check 
Skyscanner and Google flights to compare airline prices across the entire month. You can sort 
by the cheapest fairs or the fastest routes, but here's our little secret. Don't book directly 
through these websites. Once we spot the lowest fare, head over to the airlines official website 
and book directly with the airline. That way, we can avoid any third party fees. Since we're 
based in the Philippines, we usually wait for piso sales where you can grab roundtrip tickets from 
Manila for as low as 4,000 to 5,000 pesos. Yep, it's possible. And you can join my cheap flight 
alerts group on my Instagram at AngelicaWanders, where I usually share updates on airline promos 
and travel deals, so you'll be the first ones to know. But wait, before you book your flights, 
first you need to know this. When is the best time to visit Hong Kong? Well, if you want clear 
skies, lesser humidity, and weather that lets you walk around without sweating through your 
shirt, aim for November to early December. It's the most comfortable time to visit, but also 
very, very popular. So, expect heavy crowds and higher prices. For warm, sunny days, March 
to May would be ideal. The city feels lively, the flowers are blooming, and it's a great season 
for beaches and hikes. Just watch out for sudden rain and sticky humidity. Always keep an umbrella 
in your bag. Now, if you're traveling on a budget and prefer fewer tourists, January to February 
is your best bet. It's coat-worthy, the city is quieter, the lines are shorter, and you'll get a 
more local, laid-back version of Hong Kong. Okay, so now that you've booked your flight and finally 
landed at Hong Kong International Airport, don't rush out yet. Before anything, every 
tourist should tick off these three quick airport essentials. Number one, stay connected. Hong 
Kong is so much easier to navigate when you've got internet on the go. That's why I recommend 
booking an eSIM so you can immediately switch it on the moment you land. We got ours for 
only $18 with unlimited data, good for 5 days, and it made our trip hassle-free. I'll link the 
exact eSIM we used in the description below, and make sure to pre-book it in advance to get 
the best prices. Number two, get some cash. ATMs usually offer better exchange rates than airport 
money changers, so we prefer withdrawing upon arrival, but don't withdraw too much because Hong 
Kong is pretty credit card friendly. You'll only really need cash for street food stalls, night 
markets, and tiny local shops. And number three, don't forget to pick up an octopus card. This is 
your golden ticket to getting around Hong Kong on public transportation. Yes, you can also tap your 
credit card on some train stations, but it doesn't work on most buses and ferries around the city, 
so it's less reliable. That's why it's better to just grab an Octopus card and tap everywhere like 
a local. You can use it on basically everything. the MTR, which is Hong Kong Subway, buses, 
ferries, trams, even convenience stores, cafes, and some restaurants across the city. You can 
easily buy a standard Octopus card at the airport, any MTR station, or even convenience 
stores. It costs around 20 USD per card, which is $150 Hong Kong dollars. And it already 
includes $100 Hong Kong dollars stored value, plus 50 Hong Kong dollars refundable deposit. Just 
top it up, tap to ride, and you're good to go. And if you've got any leftover balance at the end 
of your trip, you can refund both your remaining balance and your deposit at any MTR service center 
or at the airport arrivals hall counter. There's also a tourist octopus card which works exactly 
the same, but it's non-refundable and cost around 5 USD. So, some people just keep it as a souvenir. 
Most travelers still prefer the standard Octopus card since you could refund your money back. 
Whichever card you use, the Google Maps app will be your best friend for navigating around 
Hong Kong. Just plug in your destination and it shows you realtime bus and train schedules. Okay, 
so now that you've got everything you need at the airport, it's time to head to the city center. 
Well, there are three main ways to getting into the city. Option one, you can take the train. 
The Hong Kong Airport Express is fast, smooth, and gets you straight to Hong Kong station in 
just 24 minutes for only $15 one way. But here's a little budget hack. You can actually get tickets 
at 25% off for only $12 if you book it online. Plus, you'll get an even bigger discount if you 
book round trip instead of one way. Don't worry, we'll leave all the discounted booking links in 
the description, so you can save a little more for some dim sum instead. Option two, you can 
take the bus if you want something cheaper and you're staying in an area a bit further from the 
train stations. The City Flyer airport buses are perfect. They're cheaper than the trains. They run 
every 15 to 20 minutes and stop at major tourist spots like Mong kok, Tsim Sha Tsui, Causeway Bay, 
and Central. Fares usually range around $5 to $7 one way and last about 45 minutes to an hour 
depending on your drop off point. Here are the most popular airport bus routes along with their 
prices and schedules. Feel free to screenshot. And the best part, you can easily tap your 
Octopus card on the bus and hop right in. Now, option three. If you've got heavy luggage or 
your flight is arriving super late, booking a taxi is a good option. They're widely available 
and metered. Plus, they're super cute. Just expect to pay around $35 to $45 one way. However, be 
aware not all drivers speak English fluently. So, it can be helpful to have your destination written 
in Cantonese or use a translator app. Otherwise, you can book rides directly using the Uber app 
and simply pin your exact destination to avoid any miscommunication. Now, if you want true zero 
stress, I highly recommend pre-booking a private airport transfer. You've got a driver waiting for 
you right at arrivals. It's surprisingly about the same price as a taxi and an Uber, and the cars 
are spacious enough for four to six people. This is perfect for families, oversized luggage, or 
anyone who wants to skip all the airport chaos entirely. But make sure you have internet to 
chat with your driver. Okay, so now you're an expert on how to get to the city. Now, it's time 
to get to your hotel. But the question is, where is the best place to stay in Hong Kong? Well, 
there are four main areas I would recommend. So, let me show you the differences between each one 
so it can help you choose the best area for you. Let's begin. First up, Tsim Sha Tsui. This is the 
best all-around area for first- timers. It's Hong Kong in full color. Harbour views, giant malls, 
street food, and museums all in one place. Plus, it connects easily to the MTR, ferries, buses, 
basically everything. Hotels range from budget to luxury, so there's something for every traveler. 
So, if you want convenience and that classic Victoria Harbour skyline, TST is the safest 
bet. Next up, Central. This is best for modern Hong Kong vibes. It's sleek, stylish, and a bit 
business-y. Packed with international restaurants, rooftop bars, and high-end malls. It's directly 
linked to the Airport Express, making arrivals and departures effortless. And you'll feel Hong 
Kong's New York energy with its glossy skyscrapers and fast-paced crowds. Perfect for travelers 
who want a modern upscale scene. Next up, Mong Kok. This is best for foodies, night markets, 
and budget stays. If you want full-on chaotic, beautiful Hong Kong energy, MongKok is it. This 
is home to lively night markets, sneakers street, cheap eats on every corner, and neon lit 
everything. It's more local, more crowded, and more colorful with tons of budget lodging 
around. So, if you're on a tight budget and love exploring night markets and street food, 
Mongkok will be your playground. And finally, Causeway Bay. This is best for shoppers and 
youthful energy. Think of it as Hong Kong's shopping paradise. Malls everywhere, bright 
billboards, busy crosswalks, and non-stop buzz. It's young, trendy, and a bit chaotic like Hong 
Kong's version of Seoul's Myeongdong with cool cafes on every corner and endless shops to 
explore. It's perfect if you want a lively, modern area packed with things to do. Of course, 
Hong Kong has plenty of other great neighborhoods, too, like Wan Chai for a more local everyday 
city vibe, or Lantau if you prefer nature, beaches, and a quieter escape. But if it's your 
first time in the city, these four areas offer the best mix of everything Hong Kong is known 
for. The city is incredibly well-connected. So, as long as you're staying near an MTR station, all 
the main tour spots are just a quick train ride away. I'll drop some affordable hotel links near 
major transport hubs, so you can get around the city easily. We personally stayed at Ocean Inn in 
Jordan, literally a 10-second walk away from the Jordan MTR and just one stop away from Mongkok. 
Our room was only $65 per night for two people. It was small, but pretty standard for budget hotels 
in Hong Kong. What mattered most was that it was clean, the location was amazing, and the reviews 
were pretty solid. They even offer a smart TV with Netflix, YouTube, and free drinking water refills 
in the lobby. Still our favorite budget hack. The AGODA app offers some of the cheapest rates, and 
you can use our coupon code WANDERSAGODA for an extra discount. Okay, so now that you've 
booked your flights, booked your hotels, and got your transport cards, let's move on to 
the next question. What are the best things to do in Hong Kong? And how much will I spend? Whether 
you're here for skyline views, epic street food, or theme parks, Hong Kong has something for 
everyone. So, let's dive in to the top 10 most popular things to do in Hong Kong, including their 
price tags. and stick around until number one because this is something you absolutely cannot 
miss. Let's start the countdown off at number 10. No Hong Kong trip is complete without a dose of 
Disney magic. Hong Kong Disneyland may be smaller than its global siblings, but that's exactly what 
makes it charming. No rushing through giant parks, no getting lost for half a day, just pure easy 
magical fun. Tickets usually go for around $80 to $100 depending on the season. And the rides are 
perfect for all ages, from adrenaline junkies to toddlers and everyone in between. You can also eat 
themed snacks, catch character meet and greets, shop for limited edition Hong Kong merch, and stay 
till the finale fireworks light up the sky. It's wholesome, heartwarming, and pure nostalgia. 
Whether you're 5 years old or a grown adult, it still hits hard when Mickey waves at you. 
Number nine, part aquarium, part theme park, and part giant zoo. Ocean Park Hong Kong 
is basically three adventures in one. From adorable pandas and playful sea lions to massive 
jellyfish and polar animals, it's like jumping between continents in a single day. Tickets cost 
$60 per head. And if you're visiting with a group, you can score discounts for three people or more 
using our discount link in the description. But here's what makes Ocean Park extra exciting. After 
riding that cliffside cable car with sweeping sea views, you're dropped off straight into a world 
of adrenaline with roller coasters that literally look like it's hanging off a cliff and a tower 
that launches you above the coastline for a view of the entire park. One minute you're saying hi 
to the cute penguins and the next minute you're clinging onto your seat and questioning your 
life's decisions. Number eight. If you're into jaw-dropping, literal head in the clouds moment, 
hop onto the Ngong Ping 360 cable car in Lantau Island. This 25-minute aerial journey glides over 
mountains, forests, and the sea, ending at the peaceful Ngong Ping village. A standard cabin cost 
about $23 one way, and the crystal cabin with a glass floor is $27. But guys, I highly recommend 
upgrading to the Crystal Plus cabin at $31. It has full 360° glass walls and a glass floor. So, 
yep, you'll be floating and seeing exactly how high you are. It's kind of scary, kind of funny, 
but honestly, we're so glad we booked this one. And here's a little budget hack. Book the 
standard cabin one way and the crystal plus cabin on the way back. You still get 
that crystal clear gondola experience, but you'll save money on half the ride. Once you 
reach the top, visit the Tian Tan Big Buddha, one of the largest outdoor seated Buddhas in the 
world, and climb up the steps for a truly majestic view. Then walk over to the Po Lin Monastery 
for some temple food, explore the village shops, and just soak in the slower mountain vibes. 
It's the perfect escape from the busy city. Number seven. If you're looking for the 
most iconic skyline view in Hong Kong, head straight to Victoria Peak, the city's highest 
point with crazy panoramic views of skyscrapers, Victoria Harbour, and lush mountains that make 
Hong Kong look like a futuristic jungle. The best way to get there is via the Peak Tram, a 
super steep funicular ride where the buildings look like they're tilted sideways because of how 
steep the track is. Roundtrip combo tickets for the Peak Tram plus the Sky Terrace 428 viewpoint 
go for around $19. And for an extra discount, you can use the coupon code AngelicaWandersKlook 
to save a little more. Once you reach the top, take in those 360° views. Snap those postcard 
worthy photos and stay until sunset to watch the skyline transform into a glittering light 
show. Number six, experience Hong Kong's best museums. Art lovers and history buffs, this one's 
for you. Start in West Kowloon at M+ Museum, a hub for contemporary visual culture. Think giant 
installations, digital art, Asian pop culture, and quirky photography. Tickets start at 
around 25 bucks, and exhibitions change often, so there's always something new to see. Then, 
right next door is the Hong Kong Palace Museum, where you can admire centuries old treasures from 
Beijing's forbidden city, gold antiques, intricate pottery, ancient paintings. It's like stepping 
into history without flying to mainland China. A standard entrance is around 9 bucks and some 
special exhibits may cost extra, but they're worth it if you're into royal treasures and dynasties. 
Number five, if there's one budget friendly must-do in Hong Kong, it's sailing across Victoria 
Harbour on the iconic Star Ferry. You can take a ride from TST to Central and it's super quick, 
just a 5 to 10 minute ride and costs less than a street snack. But the views unreal. You get front 
row seats to skyscrapers, mountain silhouettes, and Hong Kong's most famous landmarks on both 
sides of the harbour. For a full cinematic moment, go at sunset or catch the Symphony of Light Show, 
which runs every evening at 8:00 p.m. It only lasts about 10 minutes, but the entire harbor 
sparkles while you cruise through it. Cheap, scenic, iconic, and you can easily tap in and out 
with your octopus card. Hassle-free. Number four, walk the Avenue of Stars at Tsim Sha Tsui's 
waterfront. Picture the Hollywood Walk of Fame, but with a stunning waterfront backdrop. As you 
stroll along Tsim Sha Tsui's famous promenade, you'll get some of the best nighttime views in 
Hong Kong with neon reflections dancing on the water. Welcome to the Avenue of Stars, the 
city's tribute to its legendary film icons like Bruce Lee, Jackie Chan, Michelle Yeoh, and 
more. It's completely free, perfect for photos, a mix of sightseeing, and a relaxing golden hour 
walk, all in one stop. Number three, feast at a discounted hotel buffet. Hong Kong loves buffets, 
and we're not talking about sad salad bars. We're talking overflowing seafood mountains, sushi 
stations, roast meats, dim sum counters, and never ending desserts. And let me put you in on a little 
secret. Many luxury hotels offer discounted seats if you pre-book it online. And I'm talking 40 to 
50% off. You got the Marriott, Sheraton, Cordis, Harbour Grand, and many more. A lunch buffet 
at the Marriott normally costs $60 per person, but you can pre-book it online for only 30 bucks. 
We'll link down some of the best buffet deals in the description. And another budget tip, book 
weekday lunch slots. They're the cheapest, but they do fill up fast because Hong Kongers 
are serious about their buffets. Number two, one of our favorites, take a day trip to Macau. 
Just an hour by ferry or 30 minutes by bus, Macau is one of the easiest and coolest day trips 
you could take from Hong Kong. Known as the Las Vegas of Asia, it blends dazzling casino resorts 
with beautifully preserved Portuguese heritage. In a single afternoon, you can wander through gold 
lined luxury hotels with indoor gondolas. Then step into cobblestone streets surrounded by pastel 
buildings and centuries old UNESCO landmarks. Don't miss iconic spots like the ruins of St. 
Paul's, Senado Square, the colorful Taipa Village, and of course, you just have to try the famous 
egg tarts from Lord Stow's Bakery. Ferries and buses from Hong Kong cost around the same price, 
around 20 to $25 oneway. Whether you're visiting Macau for a day or staying overnight, the mix 
of glitzy city life, European architecture, and seriously amazing pastries makes Macau an easy 
win. And if you want an even deeper dive of Macau, including affordable luxury hotels and the 
best budget eats, we also have a full Macau travel guide with a 3-day budget itinerary on 
our YouTube channel. And now for number one, our ultimate favorite thing to do in Hong Kong 
is indulge in Hong Kong's street food scene. Because in Hong Kong, food isn't just food, it's 
culture. Here are eight classic Hong Kong eats you absolutely need to try. Number one, curry 
fish balls. The OG street snack. Imagine bouncy fish balls bathing in a rich spicy curry sauce. 
Every bite is loaded with flavor and you'll find them literally on every corner for around $2 per 
bowl. Number two, grab a freshly made egg waffle. Crispy on the outside, fluffy on the inside, and 
the plain ones cost around $3 each, but we always go for the chocolate filled ones at $4 from the 
Michelin recommended shop, Mammy Pancake. Number three, if you want something heavier and hardier, 
go for the clay pot rice. Smoky, comforting rice topped with Chinese sausage, tender chicken, 
and chewy bits of octopus. A combo we didn't expect to love this much. We got ours from a 
famous restaurant, Hing Kee, and their dishes cost around $12 each. Our favorite part is that 
toasted crispy rice right at the bottom of the pot. So crunchy and so so good. Number four, the 
pineapple bun. No pineapples here, just a golden crackly sugar crust sitting on top of a soft, 
fluffy bun resembling the shell of a pineapple. It's best when you get a thick slab of cold butter 
inside. For a classic experience, hit Kam Wah Cafe or just grab one from the night markets or local 
bakeries for around $2 to $3 each. Number five, wife cakes. This old school pastry is filled 
with a lightly sweet winter melon paste. Flaky on the outside, chewy on the inside, and not 
overly sweet. Individual pieces usually cost$1 to$2 dollars each and we always grab ours from Ki 
Tsui Cake Shop. Number six, you need to try Hong Kong roast duck or roast goose. Juicy, flavorful, 
and usually hanging proudly in front of the shop, so you know it's legit. Yat Lok is a famous 
Michelin starred spot known for its roasted goose, and you can get a rice meal for about $10. But if 
you're on a tighter budget, head to Cafe De Coral, a popular casual chain with affordable quick rice 
meals and all kinds of yummy Chinese eats. Their roast duck meal is around $7 and it even comes 
with hot tea. Number seven, for a true local breakfast experience, visit the Australia Dairy 
Company, and yes, it has Australia in the name, but trust me, it's a Hong Kong owned institution 
that's been running for over 50 years. The owner just used to work on a dairy farm in Australia. 
Expect silky scrambled eggs, buttery toast, ham macaroni soup, and iced milk tea. This 
full classic Hong Kong breakfast costs about $7 for the whole set. And number eight, last 
but definitely not the least, dim sum. The ultimate Hong Kong comfort food. Whether you love 
hakaw, xiao long bao, lava pao, veggie dumplings, or fried shrimp puffs, which is our personal 
weakness, the variety is endless. Each order usually ranges from $3 to $5 per basket. And 
just a tip, go with a partner or with a group of buddies so you can order everything and share 
them all. For affordable Michelin star dim sum, Tim Ho Wan is always a solid pick. Plus, their 
baked pork buns are to die for. But if you want some spots that the locals adore, try one dim 
sum, dim dim sum, or dim sum here. And yes, they all sound the same, but trust me, you can't 
go wrong with any of them. Hong Kong street food is bold, fun, and honestly part of the whole 
adventure. So, arrive hungry. And by the way, we also filmed a separate food vlog called 
the 15 must try cheap eats in Hong Kong, which hit over 250,000 views. So, if you want the 
exact restaurants and food stalls that we visited, including a map, we've linked that video in the 
description below so you only spend your calories on the good stuff. Well, now that you've seen the 
top 10 most popular tours and activities in Hong Kong, let's talk about the best ways to get around 
the city. Sure, you can always load your Octopus card for commuting on trains, buses, and ferries, 
or you could just take expensive Ubers from point to point all day. But here are a few other budget 
options that might make life easier. Option one, you can take the hop on hop off bus. If you want 
something super easy and flexible, the open top double-decker bus is your best friend. It runs 
across three different routes with 22 convenient bus stops, so you can explore at your own pace 
without stressing about directions. Simply hop on or off at any point on the route, and buses come 
every 45 to 60 minutes. An unlimited pass cost $72 for one day and $99 for 2 days. Option two, 
if you're planning a full day of sightseeing and you're tired of reloading your octopus card, you 
can grab an MTR tourist day pass, which gives you unlimited MTR rides for $10 a day. Just note, it 
does not include city buses, trams, or the airport express. Here are the lines that the pass covers. 
Feel free to screenshot. And lastly, option three, the most efficient way to explore Hong Kong and 
surprisingly affordable if you're traveling with a group. You can book a private car charter. You 
get your own dedicated driver, an 8-hour ride, and full control over your itinerary. And guess 
what? Fuel is already included, and prices go for around $322, good for six people. That's about $54 
per head, making it even cheaper than the hop-on hop-off bus. This is the ultimate budget hack if 
you're traveling with a group. Skip the commuting, skip the stress, and just enjoy your customized 
adventure. Well, now that we've covered the best areas to stay, the best things to do, and the 
best food to eat, it's time for us to put it all together and form the ultimate 5-day budget 
itinerary and cost breakdown. Feel free to save this video for future reference. So, let's 
talk pre-expenses. Before your trip begins, you generally booked your piso sale, your hotels, 
and your eSIMs in advance. Your pre-expenses would probably amount to around $298 per head. For 
day one, welcome to Hong Kong. After landing, activate your eSIM, grab an octopus card at the 
airport counter, and hop on either the airport bus or the airport express straight into the 
city. Once you've checked in and dropped your bags at your hotel, it's time to start strong. 
Head up to Victoria Peak via the Peak Tram and enjoy those insane 360 degree views from the 
Sky Terrace 428. Stay until golden hour for that dramatic Hong Kong postcard moment. Then 
after soaking it all in, head down to Temple Street or Mongkok for some street food dinner. 
Egg waffles, curry fish balls, clay pot rice, or street side dim sum. It's the perfect first 
night feast to kick off your Hong Kong adventure. The total estimated cost for day one is $70 per 
head. Now, day two is all about childhood magic. Hop onto the MTR and make your way to Hong Kong 
Disneyland. Spend the whole day jumping on rides, exploring themed lands, catching the parades, 
snacking on all the cute Disney treats, and staying for the nighttime fireworks. Then end 
your night with a comforting roast goose dinner at Yat Lok before heading back to your hotel. The 
total estimated cost for day two is $119 per head. Now for day three, it's theme park round two, 
but a totally different vibe. Head to Ocean Park for thrilling rides, marine life encounters, 
giant pandas, and cable cars. It's a full day of outdoor fun and rides. Then in the afternoon, 
make your way to the TST promenade. Stroll along the Avenue of Stars for gorgeous night views of 
Victoria Harbour. And if you want that classic Hong Kong moment, ride the Star Ferry from TST to 
Central. It's cheap, quick, and gives you front row skyline views. Then wrap up the night with the 
dinner at Dim Sum Here before heading back to your hotel. The total estimated cost for day three is 
$120 per head. It's day four and time to head to Lantau Island for one of Hong Kong's most peaceful 
experiences. But first, start your morning with a classic Hong Kong breakfast at Australia Dairy 
Company. Order their bestselling silky scrambled eggs, toast, and that comforting ham macaroni soup 
to fuel your day. Then after breakfast, take the MTR to Tung Chung station, which is the jump off 
point for the Ngong ping 360 cable car. And again, a great budget hack is to book the standard cabin 
on the way up and the Crystal Plus cabin on the way down. Once at the top, wander through Ngong 
ping village, climb the steps to the Tian Tan Big Buddha, and explore the serene Po lin monastery. 
Grab a healthy vegetarian meal, browse the village stalls, or simply enjoy the cool mountain 
air. Head back into the city and grab a yummy, budget friendly meal near your hotel and call it 
a night. The total estimated cost for day four is $92 per head. And lastly, day five. For your 
final day, start in West Kowloon at the M+ Museum, home to modern art and digital installations. 
Then stroll over next door to the Hong Kong Palace Museum to see beautifully curated treasures from 
China's dynasties. After that, treat yourself to a good last day lunch. You can go for some classic 
dim sum because you can never go wrong with dim sum. Or if you want one last semi- splurge, 
enjoy a luxury hotel buffet. But of course, make sure to pre-book it online for that sweet 
50% off discount. Seafood towers, sushi stations, roast meats, and endless desserts. Then spend 
the rest of your afternoon shopping. Causeway Bay or Central for fashion and luxury. Mongkok 
or Jordan for quirky snacks and bargain finds. or Dondon Donki, which is Hong Kong's version of Don 
Quijote if you miss Japan style discount shopping. And finally, when it's time to head out, grab 
your luggages from your hotel, take the airport bus or airport express back to the airport. But 
before entering departures, don't forget to refund your Octopus card at the airport arrivals hall to 
get your remaining balance and deposit back. Then board your flight and wave a little goodbye to 
Hong Kong. The total estimated cost for day 5 is $72 per head. If you add all of it together, a 
5-day 4-night Hong Kong adventure would be, drum roll please, $771 per head. This estimated cost 
already covers everything. Flights, hotel, food, transportation, activities, and entrance fees. 
Imagine it already includes major highlights like Disneyland, Ocean Park, Victoria Peak, and even 
that Crystal Plus cabin going to Ngong ping. Think of this itinerary as the ultimate yet practical 
guide for budgetarians who want to experience the very best of Hong Kong without breaking 
the bank. And if you have a few extra days, consider taking a quick side trip to Macau since 
it's just an hour away. And don't forget, we also have a detailed Macau travel guide on our YouTube 
channel if you want all the tips and tricks on how to travel Macau on a budget. On this channel, we 
teach you guys how to save money when traveling, how to book cheap flights, how to book cheap 
hotels, and just a whole lot of travel hacks. So, if this travel guide helped you in any way, please 
hit that like button, hit that subscribe button, and comment down below if you have any questions 
about Hong Kong. We do our best to reply to every single comment, so don't be afraid to ask. We 
hope you guys enjoyed this travel guide, and we hope you enjoy your trip to Hong Kong. This is 
Angelica and Aileen Wanders. Never stop wandering! [Music]

### [Great Value for Money! A Custom Tailoring Shop Worth Considering for Everyone](https://www.tripadvisor.com/ShowUserReviews-g294217-d34002343-r1039079770-Etailor-Hong_Kong.html)

That afternoon during my trip, I walked into this custom tailoring shop with a bit of anticipation, hoping to get a custom-made shirt. As soon as I entered, the shop owner warmly greeted me and began explaining the shop's specialties in detail. Their expertise lies in custom-tailored suits, shirts, and overcoats. They also have a good range of women's wear, though I didn't look closely at those this time. The owner told me they are very particular about their craftsmanship. They provide professional advice based on individual body types. After taking measurements, they let you choose your preferred fabric. If there happens to be a ready-made garment in the shop, she'll even bring it over specially for you to see as a reference, helping you make the most satisfactory choice. I chose a white shirt with a blue check, a standard professional style. The owner's service attitude was excellent, making me feel both professionally cared for and personally attended to. ### [Highly Prone Toward Overcrowding](https://www.tripadvisor.com/ShowUserReviews-g294217-d624841-r1053974604-Ngong_Ping_360-Hong_Kong.html)

(Visited on November 23, 2025) Eighteen years ago (exactly in 2007), I used to go to Tian Tan Buddha. Unfortunately, Ngong Ping was temporary closed down due to mismanagement issue. So I ended up went to Tian Tan Buddha by a minibus. Fast forward, finally I got the chance to take a ride to Tian Tan Buddha by cable car. There were several lifts to access the station. I came at around 10 AM. At that time, the queue wasn't very long. So I didn't need to wait for long time. The lane at the station was uphill. Fortunately, the station use ramp, thus very friendly for wheelchair user. Shortly after enter the cable, a photographer came to take a photo for each passenger. The photo can be purchased after passenger arrive at the destination, if desired. Passenger can refuse to get photographed politely. After returned from Ngong Ping, passenger across gift shop area. Not quite large, but offered plenty amounts of souvenirs. The station is highly prone toward overcrowding during high season, particularly in daytime. This because of the station wasn't very large. The queue could be very long to the point the waiting time reach 1 hour. Based on personal experience, came to the station as early as possible before daytime. ### [10,000 Buddhas](https://www.tripadvisor.com/ShowUserReviews-g294217-d593160-r1039568338-Ten_Thousand_Buddhas_Monastery_Man_Fat_Sze-Hong_Kong.html)

The 10,000 Buddhas Monastery is located north of central Hong Kong, accessible via the Blue Line and Sha Tin subway stop. Once out of the subway, you have to take a side path and then walk along a shopping center before you can take a passage (where cleanliness leaves something to be desired) to climb the stairs leading to the monastery. Along the way are erected golden statues that accompany you on your ascent. Once you reach the top, a large square welcomes you with a pagoda, statues and various halls where you can gather. The monastery is not in one of the most beautiful areas of Hong Kong but it is open daily from 9am to 5.30pm and access is free. Allow 1h time to go up and down and see the different buildings. ### [Quality shopping mall in Sha Tin](https://www.tripadvisor.com/ShowUserReviews-g294217-d2166000-r1029480838-New_Town_Plaza-Hong_Kong.html)

This is a popular shopping mall in Sha Tin with direct access by way of the Sha Tin MTR Station. There is a good range of mid-range to luxury brand name retailers here along with a blend of Chinese and Western eateries, coffee shops and bakeries. You'll also find a useful international supermarket, cinema, Snoopy World (free entry) for the kiddos. New Town Plaza is a good place to grab a bite to eat or coffee after sightseeing around Sha Tin. There are several places that open for breakfast including bakehouse who opened a shop here earlier this year. There are often interesting mall displaces, particularly around the holidays. Some are noticeably more interesting than others but always worth a look if visiting these times of the year. ### [Nice boat, rude staff, and uninviting behavior towards foreign visitors](https://www.tripadvisor.com/ShowUserReviews-g294217-d2282452-r1044553059-TurboJet-Hong_Kong.html)

We went 2 hours early to book the tickets, but they told us that economy seats were all sold out. Staff at ticket counter was abrupt and didn't speak English. When translated in Cantonese, they pretended to not understand. We ended up buying upper class ticket which was double the price. We were not able to select seats at the ticketing counter. TIP for upper class or higher class ticket: After finishing immigration, when u get to the guy who checks your ticket, ask him to give your seat assignment. Make sure he gives you good window seat. Tell him to show u the seat map so you can pick the seats. The person is usually rude/abrupt, but don't let him rush you. Also, there is separate boarding line for upper class ticket holders. Boarding process is chaotic and not organized. Staff pn the boat is also rude. They won't let you walk around the boat even while it is docked. The deck is NOT opened to public to go on. You have to stay seated the entire time which defeats the whole purpose of enjoying the boat ride. There is barely any difference between economy and upper class... just not worth it, except for a bag peanuts, free water bottle, and meat sandwich which was useless for us as vegetarians. No veggie option which I think is unacceptable. Strongly recommend booking early and online to avoid lines. Do NOT pay extra for higher class tickets. If staff is rude, ignore them and stick to your guns. They always want tourists to spend more than what they should be, so be a smart traveller!   Read more    [Review of: TurboJet](https://www.tripadvisor.com/Attraction_Review-g294217-d2282452-Reviews-TurboJet-Hong_Kong.html)Visited January 2026 Traveled as a couple  Written January 1, 2026

## Hong Kong travel tips

Powered by AI

These questions and answers were created by AI, using the most common questions travelers ask in the forums.

What are the transportation options from Hong Kong Airport?

#### Public Transport Options

From Hong Kong Airport, travelers have multiple transportation options. Some travelers suggest taking the Airport Express train to Hong Kong Station and then a taxi to the final destination. This is considered fast and less costly than a direct taxi from the airport. The Airport Express operates until shortly before 1 am and resumes around 6 am.

#### Taxi Service

For those preferring a direct route, taxis are available 24/7 at the airport. It is recommended to carry cash as few taxis accept credit cards. Travelers can choose from red taxis for most destinations in Hong Kong, green taxis for the northern territories, and blue taxis for Lantau Island.

#### Bus Service

Airport buses are another economical option, with comprehensive coverage across the city. The bus routes A11 and NA11 are specifically mentioned as convenient for reaching central parts of Hong Kong. These buses offer a balance of cost and convenience, with stops close to major districts and hotels.

#### Additional Considerations

Travelers also noted the convenience of using an Octopus card for easy payment on public transport, including buses and the Airport Express. This card can be purchased at the airport.

What are the check-in and transfer procedures at Hong Kong Airport?

#### Check-in Procedures

According to forum posts, check-in procedures at Hong Kong Airport can vary based on whether passengers have a single or separate tickets and if they are flying with the same airline. For passengers with separate tickets, they must often exit the secure area to check in for their onward flight at the airline's counter when early check-in is not available.

#### Transfer Procedures

Travelers have called out that transfer procedures at Hong Kong Airport are generally smooth, especially for those with carry-on luggage only. For those with checked luggage, the process might require retrieving bags and rechecking them unless the bags are tagged through to the final destination. A transit desk is available for passengers who need assistance with their boarding passes or transfers between flights.

#### Online Check-in and Boarding Passes

Some travelers mentioned the possibility of online check-in and obtaining mobile or printed boarding passes to facilitate easier transfers. This can allow passengers to bypass the check-in counter and proceed directly to security and the departure gate.

#### Luggage Tagging

For travelers with checked luggage, ensuring that bags are tagged through to the final destination at the initial check-in is crucial. Failure to do so might require passengers to go through immigration and customs to retrieve and recheck their luggage, complicating the transfer process.

What are the visa requirements for Hong Kong?

#### Visa Requirements Overview

According to forum posts, the visa requirements for Hong Kong vary based on nationality. For instance, Namibian citizens can stay visa-free for up to 90 days, while Sri Lankans need a visa in advance for entering Hong Kong, mainland China, and Macau. U.S. passport holders are allowed visa-free entry for up to 90 days.

#### Specific Cases

Some travelers mentioned that Vietnamese nationals, including U.S. permanent residents, require visas to enter Hong Kong. Additionally, Bangladeshi passport holders must obtain a visa even if they are just transiting and staying airside at the airport.

#### Additional Resources

For the most accurate and detailed information, travelers have called out the official Hong Kong Immigration Department website. Links to this site were repeatedly suggested for checking specific visa requirements and applying for the necessary travel documents.

What are the uses and benefits of the Octopus card in Hong Kong?

#### General Use

According to forum posts, the Octopus card is a versatile payment method accepted across various transport modes including MTR, buses, ferries, and trams in Hong Kong. It also facilitates purchases at convenience stores, vending machines, and some taxis.

#### Convenience and Efficiency

Travelers have highlighted the convenience of not needing to carry cash or change as the Octopus card allows for quick and easy tap-and-go payments. This feature is particularly appreciated for its efficiency when transferring between different transport modes.

#### Cost Benefits

Some users pointed out that while the Octopus card itself does not offer fare discounts, it eliminates the need to buy single-ride tickets each time, which can be more time-consuming and sometimes more expensive. The card can be topped up as needed, and any remaining balance along with the deposit is refundable upon return of the card.

#### Souvenir Option

For many visitors, the Tourist Octopus card is a popular souvenir option, providing a physical memento of their trip to Hong Kong, albeit at a higher upfront cost compared to the standard on-loan Octopus card.

What are specific festivities during Chinese New Year in Hong Kong?

#### Street Markets and Decorations

According to forum posts, during the Chinese New Year (CNY), street markets such as Tai Yuen Street in Wanchai and Fuk Wing Street in Sham Shui Po offer New Year decorations. These markets likely start selling festive items even before the official celebrations begin.

#### Flower Markets

Travelers have mentioned that flower markets are operational around CNY. These markets are particularly vibrant and are a traditional part of the festivities, offering an array of flowers that symbolize good luck and prosperity.

#### Parades and Carnivals

Some travelers noted that the traditional Chinese New Year Night Parade in Tsim Sha Tsui, which features floats and performances, is a major attraction. However, in recent years, this parade has been replaced by the Cathay Pacific International Chinese New Year Carnival held at the West Kowloon Cultural District, offering stage entertainment, street entertainers, and food booths.

#### Public Celebrations

Posts highlight that many public celebrations such as lion dances, fairs, and various scheduled events take place across the city, contrary to the belief that CNY is not publicly celebrated. These festivities provide a rich cultural experience reflecting local traditions and customs.

What are the must-see attractions in Hong Kong?

#### Popular Attractions

According to forum posts, some of the must-see attractions in Hong Kong include Victoria Peak for its stunning city views, the bustling Temple Street Night Market, and the serene Chi Lin Nunnery. The iconic Star Ferry ride across Victoria Harbour is also highly recommended for a picturesque view of the city's skyline.

#### Cultural and Historical Sites

Travelers have called out the Tian Tan Buddha and the nearby Tai O fishing village as significant cultural spots. The historic Ping Shan Heritage Trail offers a glimpse into Hong Kong's past, and the Symphony of Lights is a spectacular nightly multimedia show that highlights the city's famous skyline.

#### Outdoor Activities

For those interested in outdoor activities, forum users suggest visiting the Dragon's Back trail for hiking, which provides scenic views of Hong Kong's natural landscape. The Hong Kong Geopark, known for its geological formations, and the tranquil Hong Kong Wetland Park are also popular among nature enthusiasts.

What is the typical weather like in Hong Kong?

#### General Climate

Hong Kong experiences a subtropical climate. According to forum posts, the region is generally hot and humid from mid-April to early October. Winters are mild with temperatures ranging comfortably for most visitors.

#### Seasonal Variations

From October to April, the weather tends to be more stable and cooler, making it an ideal time for visitors aiming to avoid excessive heat and humidity. December specifically is highlighted as dry and sunny, with very low chances of typhoons or severe weather disruptions.

#### Rain Patterns

Rainfall in Hong Kong varies by season. June is noted as the rainiest month, often involving short bursts of rain rather than continuous downpours. July also sees significant rain, aligning with the broader rainy season, which can extend from late spring into early fall.

#### Temperature Ranges

During the cooler months, temperatures can vary widely. In early December, for example, temperatures typically range from 17°C to 21°C, providing a comfortable climate for outdoor activities. Conversely, summer months are characterized by higher temperatures and increased humidity, making it less comfortable for outdoor excursions.

results.title: 15 Incredible Things to Do in Hong Kong: The 2026 Edition
results.content:
As the newer side of Hong Kong, Kowloon combines a wide range of international culture and modern amenities. It showcases how the city is often seen on the world stage, a cosmopolitan city and a thriving commercial hub.

The district’s main street is Nathan Road. Here, you’ll find rows of trendy restaurants serving anything from hearty kebabs to traditional eats. Between the restaurants are independent fashion outlets and several malls. For high-end shopping, make your way down Canton Road.

Other major attractions in the area include the iconic Clock Tower and the Hong Kong Space Museum. Aside from it being a hub of food and great shopping, Tsim Sha Tsui is one of the best places to base yourself throughout your Hong Kong experience.

## Relax in Hong Kong Park [...] Here, it’s common for locals to offer rides along the tidal flats and see the village from their perspective. Afterwards, head to the local market, where you’ll be able to try some fresh catch long before it makes its way to the big city.

Aside from experiencing the fishing side of life, slightly inland you’ll discover a lovely community and some of the best street food going around. Some of the must try treats included salted fish and the Tai O Husband. The latter is a pizza roll of shrimp and pork.

## Climb Up to Big Buddha

Tian Tan Buddha in Hong Kong
Tian Tan Buddha in Hong Kong [...] ## Visit Wong Tai Sin Temple

In Kowloon, the Wong Sai Tin Temple was one of the more recent additions to Hong Kong’s list of spiritual sites. The first iteration of this temple was developed at the beginning of the 20th century, however, it was entirely replaced by a new temple in 1968.

The temple celebrates Wong Tai Sin, a revered Taoist god who is thought to bring good luck and help heal the sick. Visitors can explore the colorful grounds, home to many sites such as the beautiful Good Wish Garden and the Hall of Three Saints. The centerpiece, however, is an expansive hall that honors Confucius along with his dozens of disciples. As you wander inside, you will pass several fortune-tellers alongside rows of joss sticks and incense candles emitting a powerful aroma throughout the hall.
